speaker: interviewer
text: I'm just going to stick around with this [INAUDIBLE]. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We are now in the small village of Plotzke. That's P-L-O-umlaut-T-Z-K-E. This is the town, the second stop we made on our-- a death march. Uh, as I've mentioned before, the 9th of April, the time must have been probably somewhere between 10:00 and 11:00 at night. We walked perhaps five, six kilometers from the last stop. The house in the background which can be seen, I hope some. Because the atmospheric conditions have changed since we started speaking.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The house in the background, as I've mentioned before, is very much vivid in my memory. It's-- I'm almost sure-- the same house. If not, it's another one probably just like it. In this particular square of town, where I made my break-- and the one above, the good Lord, was with me-- we stopped here briefly for a drink of water. And-- and the lady came out in the-- in the doorway with a bucket of water and a ladle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as we went to get a drink of water, I quickly pushed the water and her bucket aside and stuck my head between her legs, and wanted to get into the hallway, which is-- of course, inside the house. She, of course, was surprised and kept telling me, What are you doing? You're going to get us both killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was too late by then. And I told her, please be quiet. I-- I need a shelter. And I found my way in the dark, touching the walls of on the stairway and went to the upstairs-- to the upstairs of the house, where I have looked down. And I saw the rest of-- of the inmates taking a break. And I couldn't believe I was away from the column. If they find me now, they sure will kill me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was trying to make myself-- my way to the attic, which was above the-- above the second floor. By that time, I had two more guys behind me, in [? their ?] wooden shoes, stepping on me, on the steps.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were rushing up to the attic. And I-- I told them, I said, Wait. I want to get up there too. They rushed to the attic, pushed the trap door from the attic, and jumped up there. And as I slowly made my way up there, they closed it behind me, and I couldn't make it to the attic.

speaker: interviewer
text: And by that time I heard the guards saying, someone is there! [GERMAN]. Which means, You got a flashlight? Quick, I hear someone there! So they come in and look in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, I was terrified. I figure, this is it. This is the end. I couldn't make it to the attic. And I could not run down. I couldn't jump out the window. It would probably break my-- both of my legs. And they would probably kill me. So I found, in the landing between the two-- two floors, like God would send me-- send it to me, a-- a sack of-- of-- of-- of potato peels. I quickly emptied the potato peels, Crawled into the sack, pushed myself into the corner, covered my face. My-- my back was bare and exposed. But the bag was in a corner of the little hallway, so they couldn't see me. And I could hear them running up the steps, with the-- with this flashlight, which shone on the sack. Oh, the potato sack, I could see through little holes. And they tun straight to the attic. All I heard after that is shouts of [GERMAN]. Don't shoot! Don't shoot!

speaker: interviewer
text: Two or three shots rang out. And they killed them both. And now they're coming down, talking to themselves. Was there any more? The other guy says, No, I don't-- I don't believe so, he says. So they knocked on the door to the left and knocked-- and also to the right there. Did you see anybody here?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the people said, No, no, no, there's nobody here. And, of course, I was in the potato sack. And I figured, this is going to be my moment now, and I am next. And as they came to the landing, I was crouched and I was sitting in a crouched position. I remember today, yes, vividly, the coat, the tail coats of the-- of the guard brushed my knees. And I know-- I know he touched me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I quickly said Shma Yisrael' which is a prayer that we say, Shma Yisrael, Adonai eloheinu. Hear, Israel, Lord our God, the Lord is one. And all of a sudden, they were walking down the steps, and they didn't see me. And I'm pinching myself. I can't believe-- is this-- is this really true? At this-- this place, here? I'm-- I'm saved at this-- this? Thank you. They-- they passed by me, didn't see me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I was-- I sat there, frozen, as I heard them walk down the steps. And outside, they were already screaming, [GERMAN]! Which means information, get in formation quick. Which means marching. I sure am cold here. Forward, march. They are already calling it to-- to-- to-- to get to follow my in the columns of five and start marching. And I waited until I heard the last steps going down this street. When I didn't hear any more wooden shoes and steps, I waited for another 15, 20 minutes. And I ventured to get out the sack of-- of my potato sack.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I knocked on the door of the guy who told them that no one was here. And I said, please, can you help me? And he looked at me, in my striped uniform. And he says in Polish, he says [POLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: You're going to get us all killed, he said. What are you doing here, my God! I says, please, sir. It turns out, he was a Polish worker of forced-- of forced labor. And I says, please help me. I'm you-- I'm your brother. I'm also from Poland. Please help me. I-- I-- you can see who I am. I just ran away. Give me some clothes. He says-- he told me he didn't have any clothes himself. But he did give me a coat. So at least I had a coat. I still had striped pants. But at least I had a coat, which was probably three or four sizes too large. And I thanked him. And I get-- got out of the house, looking for a place to hide.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Say when. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I-- as I ventured out of my potato sack, I couldn't believe. I was pinching myself. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this-- is this happening to me? Am I actually free? And I thought, gosh, [? those ?] Americans will probably be here the following day. Because the bridge behind us was blown up already.

speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I figured, if I could just hide tonight. So I walked down the street. And I found a-- a barn in the back of a house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I opened the barn. And a chicken begin to cackle. And a dog began to bark. And I quickly ran inside the barn and shut the door. You know, went on up the ladder. And found a haystack in the top in a barn, in the hay loft. I buried myself in the haystack and fell asleep. I can't-- I have to just tell one more scene. When I woke up the next day, I didn't know where I was. After-- after four years of-- of-- of-- of being watched over every-- a guard with-- with guns and and dogs, all of a sudden, I was a free-- I thought I was a free person. This was the 9th of April. The morning was the 10th of April. And I think it's, gosh, that we're going to be liberated the next day. But it wasn't until four weeks later, the Russians came. And I was hiding out in places like barns, horse stables, and hunting for food at night. Later I found some Russians and Polish people who helped me to-- get-- with some food they would bring to me at night. And because I had no papers, no-- no money, no clothes. And, of course, I was Jewish, in a-- in a-- in a German town-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --where-- where they were still killing people. And what happened to the rest of my fellow inmates, I have no-- no idea. But I were able to dig up some data on it run by the Magdeburg Jewish community. We started at 1,500 people marching from Schonebeck. As of 24 days later, 300 arrive at the final destination, something like 50 kilometers farther east, northeast from-- of here. And of course, they were also liberated then, by the Russians, run over by the Russian army. And so, 300 actually made it through out of 1,500-some prisoners. And until today, I'm thankful-- I'm thankful to the one above that I did make it to this house. And this was my saving place. And again, I want to thank the Foundation for the opportunity of being able to-- enabling me to tell this story. Although in some unusual circumstances, with atmospheric conditions being dark and cold. However, my story is, nevertheless, chilling, but yet a heartwarming story. So again, thanks for the opportunity. And let us remember, we got to keep telling this story while we're alive. Keep-- keep witnesses. Please, we have to tell what happened. This is my small contribution to-- to the part of my own saving-- savior and liberation. Thank you so much. God bless you all. And let us continue.
